full_transcript.text: So Ricky Rivas, congratulations, you've won 3K. We'll be in touch. Uh, I know we, I know we've got him on X, so we'll find him. And Lemon Slice AI and Bo, $1,000 each. Congratulations to you guys as well.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Wow, look at that, the audience goes crazy. Um, here's what I'd like to do as well. Uh, if—okay, when we give prizes like this, you gotta pay taxes, uh, to the best of my knowledge. So we're probably going to need to get you to fill out like a little tax form, because it's over $600.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: We'll put him in touch with Heidi, she'll figure it all out.

full_transcript.speaker: Skyler Chan
full_transcript.text: So yeah, we'll figure it out. Uh, we'll send it to you. And then, um, it's all open source, so great. Uh, you guys open sourced it and if you build it further you own it, you know, I'm not taking any claim to ownership here. But I really like it if I hope they join us for the annotated project as well and do that bounty next. We'll probably take like a month or two to run through that one and do the similar process but even better. I love this bounty program because it's fun, it shows the creativity, we get to give feedback on it. And, uh, if you don't claim your prize, I will either rotate it into a future bounty or I'll donate it to the charity of your choice as long as it's not something crazy. Like just pick some normal charity.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Oh, what a sport.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Yeah, don't pick a normal charity.

full_transcript.speaker: Skyler Chan
full_transcript.text: I don't want anybody to think I rug—that I rug pulled anybody Lon or that I do this in a front fair manner.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: No, I think we did this in a perfectly—it took us a little while. It took us longer than I think we'd hoped it was going to take, but a lot of people entered. We had to install and check out a lot of apps, we wanted to be thorough and very fair, not just give it to the first good one that we saw, but take a holistic view. So, yeah, I think we'll take a little bit longer. And you can go to thisweekinstartups.com/bounty if you want to keep an eye on the active bounties that we're looking for and—and jump in. Uh, and yeah, the next one that we want to do is for this annotated app. I will, uh, give you a little taste here. annotated.com. It's an extension for clipping and reacting to content from any website. So you see some text, you see some audio, there's some video that you want. So users can annotate any piece of content like that. It then creates a landing page with a link to a news story or the post, and the people can react with that annotation on that landing page. So, you know, think of a sort of a variation on something like Reddit where you're creating a topic that then becomes a whole thread that people can comment on. But instead of it just being you type into Reddit, you're sort of taking a piece of content from elsewhere on the web and that is jumpstarting the conversation. I was saying before Jason, this reminds me a lot of an old-school service called Delicious that I used to love many, many years ago, uh, which was also kind of a similar concept. Like find a quote or a good blog post and then you pull it in, bookmark it and then other people can see what you've bookmarked and comment on it. This is sort of the next level of that I think.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Yeah, I'm—I'm really excited about this because I bought annotated.com I think 20 years ago for 30 or 40k. And that was going to be my next startup, uh, but I went with Mahalo instead. So, uh, my concept here is that we lose a lot of the—this is like cruft and like the dead web, you know, like things die. And so what I want to have happen here, I'm just going to unpack Annotated a little bit if I may. You're on a New York Times story. Just came out, it's controversial. You're the subject of that story, right? You highlight your quote, and then you reply and say I didn't say that.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: or I said this, but they left out the second thing, here's my screenshot of what I actually said to that person and now you have this permanent object and I'm not including blockchain in this, but a permanent object, so that if they go change the quote, like let's say in that New York Times story or they got a fact wrong and you fact-checked it and you put it there, that object exists forever as a moment in time. Let's say there's a video and it is the promo video for this new product that's coming out, right? full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Yes.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: You take that John Stewart clip, you do it, you say hey look at he was proven right, here's all the facts, right? The object is the URL, but we will take the, you know, let's say up to 30 seconds of the original video, we'll downgrade it to like lower so you can do fair use. full_transcript.speaker: Skyler Chan
full_transcript.text: And it's the linking back, I think. full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: I used to want people to get permission before they clipped, I've changed my position on it. If you do commentary, that's fair use. full_transcript.speaker: Skyler Chan

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: the show that they're doing and the great content that they're making, so nobody, yeah, I don't think anybody really objects.

full_transcript.text: Sure.

highlights: Gruv Space is developing an on-site lunar factory that converts moon soil, or regolith, into building bricks using robotic arms and geopolymer binding. This technology enables the construction of lunar infrastructure like hotels and bases without the prohibitive cost of transporting heavy building materials from Earth.

Space tourism serves as a commercial "wedge" to fund the development of critical civilizational technologies, such as habitation modules and resource utilization. Establishing these capabilities on the moon is considered a necessary stepping stone for the eventual goal of human survival and independence on Mars.

To prevent being crippled by government intervention or technical downtime, businesses are encouraged to build an "AI harness" that allows for the dynamic routing of tasks across multiple language models. This strategy avoids a single point of failure and ensures that a company’s AI infrastructure remains operational regardless of which specific provider is currently available.

The recent government restriction on advanced AI models highlights an unprecedented tension where the executive branch may intercede to pull private technology from the market over national security and "cyber weapon" concerns. This shift signals a new era where AI developers must prioritize self-regulation to prevent emergency government shutdowns of their products.

The "Annotated" project proposes a way to create permanent, unchangeable records of digital content and public reactions to prevent the "dead web" or the silent alteration of historical quotes. By using fair-use clips for commentary, users can create a transparent historical record of digital media that remains accessible even if the original publisher changes the source material.
summary:
This episode of *This Week in Startups* features an interview with Skyler Chan, founder of Gruv Space, who details a futuristic plan to build the first lunar hotel and base using local moon soil, or "regolith." By employing robots and a specialized geopolymer-based brick-making factory, the company aims to eliminate the prohibitive costs of shipping construction materials from Earth. Chan outlines a strategic roadmap that begins with achieving NASA's "Technology Readiness Levels" for base construction and eventually expands into high-end space tourism, positioning the moon as a critical infrastructure stepping stone for humanity's eventual migration to Mars.

The discussion shifts to a developing controversy between the AI firm Anthropic and the U.S. government regarding the restricted access to advanced Claude models, "Fable" and "Mythos." Hosts Jason Calacanis and Lon Harris debate whether the government’s intervention is a necessary precaution against "jailbroken" AI being used for cyber warfare or a result of political friction between the administration and Anthropic’s leadership. This segment highlights broader themes of AI sovereignty and national security, specifically the danger of a "single point of failure" and how international reliance on U.S. models is driving a global push for localized, open-source AI alternatives.

Finally, the show highlights the intersection of AI and media through the conclusion of a $5,000 developer bounty for a real-time podcast companion. "My AI Sidecast" by Ricky Rivas was awarded the top prize for its ability to provide live fact-checking and commentary during recordings. Building on this momentum, Calacanis introduces his next incubation project, Annotated.com, which aims to create a permanent, verifiable layer for web content through "fair use" clipping and commentary. The episode concludes with a look at the hosts' evolving strategy of using these bounty programs to identify talent and incubate new startups directly within their venture firm.
